bibliographic_data.title: ColBERT's [MASK]-based Query Augmentation: Effects of Quadrupling the Query Input Length
bibliographic_data.abstract: A unique aspect of ColBERT is its use of [MASK] tokens in queries to score documents (query augmentation). Prior work shows [MASK] tokens weighting non-[MASK] query terms, emphasizing certain tokens over others, rather than introducing whole new terms as initially proposed. We begin by demonstrating that a term weight behavior previously reported for [MASK] tokens in ColBERTv1 holds for ColBERTv2. We then examine the effect of changing the number of [MASK] tokens from zero to up to four times past the query input length used in training, both for first stage retrieval, and for scoring candidates, observing an initial decrease in performance with few [MASK]s, a large increase when enough [MASK]s are added to pad queries to an average length of 32, then a plateau in performance afterwards. Additionally, we compare baseline performance to performance when the query length is extended to 128 tokens, and find that differences are small (e.g., within 1% on various metrics) and generally statistically insignificant, indicating performance does not collapse if ColBERT is presented with more [MASK] tokens than expected.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper examines the effect of increasing the number of [MASK] tokens in queries on ColBERT's performance, demonstrating that a term weighting behavior previously reported for [MASK] tokens in ColBERTv1 holds for ColBERTv2.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors changed the number of [MASK] tokens from zero to up to four times past the query input length used in training and observed the impact on performance during both first stage retrieval and scoring candidates. They also compared baseline performance with a 128-token query length.
outcomes: An initial decrease in performance with few [MASK]s, followed by an increase when enough [MASK]s are added to pad queries to an average length of 32 tokens.
A plateau in performance after adding more than four times the number of [MASK]s ColBERT has been trained with.
Small differences (within 1%) and generally statistically insignificant performance changes when comparing baseline performance to a 128-token query length.
core_content.full_text_markdown:
ColBERT’s use of multiple token embedding vectors supports fine-grained matching between queries and documents. The model ranks documents by adding the maximum similarity of a document token embedding to each query token embedding, as shown in Equation 1. This greedy alignment of query to document token embeddings has been dubbed MaxSim.

The paper examines the effect of increasing the number of [MASK] tokens in queries on ColBERT's performance. The authors demonstrate that a term weighting behavior previously reported for [MASK] tokens in ColBERTv1 holds for ColBERTv2. They changed the number of [MASK] tokens from zero to up to four times past the query input length used in training and observed the impact on performance during both first stage retrieval and scoring candidates. Additionally, they compared baseline performance with a 128-token query length.

The results show an initial decrease in performance with few [MASK]s, followed by an increase when enough [MASK]s are added to pad queries to an average length of 32 tokens. After adding more than four times the number of [MASK]s ColBERT has been trained with, there is a plateau in performance. Comparing baseline performance to a 128-token query length shows small differences (within 1%) and generally statistically insignificant changes.
